## Summary

**Worktree:** `/Users/pablofernandez/src/tenex-edge-fabric` on branch **`fabric-architecture`** (11 commits, branched from your committed `from_session` WIP + wiki docs on `master`). **Not merged** — it's a worktree as you asked.

**Orchestration:** I drove the phases sequentially (they're a hard dependency chain), fanning out background agents only where files were genuinely independent — Phase 0 split into 2 parallel build units (integration-binary vs lib). The other phases concentrate in `state.rs`/`server.rs`, so they ran single-agent; I re-ran the full test ladder myself at every join rather than trusting agent reports, and committed each phase as a rollback point.

| Phase | What landed |
|---|---|
| 0 | 20 `freeze_*` regression tests pinning routing/dedup/ACL/context/idempotency |
| 1 | Canonical schema (8 tables) + accessors + idempotent backfill |
| 2 | `StoreReader`/`StoreWriter` split; readers go through read-model methods |
| 3 | `src/fabric/*`: `RawEnvelope`/`Scope`/`NostrDelivery`/`Kind1WireCodec`; filters out of the codec |
| 4 | `handle_incoming` → thin dispatch to `fabric::materialize` |
| 5 | `Kind1Nip29Provider` bundles delivery/codec/materializer/lifecycle; single-writer preserved |
| 6 | `SendIntent`/`provider.send`/`sync_state`; dual-write canonical messages (in + out) |
| 7 | Thread/message reads (`threads` CLI + 3 RPCs); `--thread` reply via NIP-10 root tag |
| 8 | Removed `Codec::filters`/`SubScope`; group builders → fabric; backfill wired at startup; readers name no kinds |

**Verification:** Full ladder green at every join and final — **120 lib + 4 mechanics + 14 integration + 1 e2e, 0 rustc warnings, 0 net-new clippy lints**. Plus a real end-to-end smoke (the advisor's catch): daemon starts clean and **startup backfill migrates a *populated* db correctly** (the corruption-motivated path), and the new `threads` command works CLI→RPC.

**Honest limitations** (stated, not papered over):
- **39000/39002 inbound materialization** is frozen only at the store-primitive level — the nak harness can't emit relay-authored group events, so the relay→materializer dispatch (rewritten in Phase 4) isn't driven end-to-end. Covered by the moved unit tests + the e2e group-create test.
- The canonical read model is **written but largely unread** — inbox/who/turn-start stay on legacy tables by *deliberate retention* (per the doc's §6 escape hatch). The "new fabric = no reader edits" goal holds because the shared materializer still writes legacy `inbox`; it's not a full read-model cutover.
- `--thread` reply is verified at the component level (tag-encode + inbound grouping), not as one live send→receive→group round-trip.
- The live external `nip29_probe` (`wss://nip29.f7z.io`) wasn't run — it probes relay rules, not this refactor.

## I ran live `claude` + `codex` through the refactored daemon

1. **claude** ran `tenex-edge send-message --recipient <codex-pubkey> --message "…review the auth refactor…"`
2. **codex** caught it up, then `tenex-edge send-message … --thread <id>` (a real `--thread` reply)
3. **claude** read it back with the new `tenex-edge threads` command

The conversation landed correctly in the **canonical read model** — both messages grouped in one thread via the shared NIP‑10 root, correct authors and recipients:

```
fafe9620 (claude) outbound  Hey codex, can you review the auth refactor…
819adf51 (codex)  outbound  Reviewed the auth refactor, looks solid… Ship it.   ← same thread
```

## The live run found 3 real bugs the cargo tests missed — now fixed

This is exactly why you pushed for real e2e:

- **A** — `add_message_recipient` wasn't idempotent for untargeted (NULL `target_session`) recipients: SQLite treats NULL as distinct in the PK, so every relay echo / catch-up refetch appended a duplicate row (I saw **9 rows** for one message). Fixed with a NULL-case existence guard + regression test.
- **B** — `threads --project` printed a **truncated** thread id you couldn't pass back to `--thread`. Now prints the full id.
- **C** — `threads --thread <unknown>` returned `"daemon returned neither ok nor error"` because `rpc_thread_meta` returned a bare JSON `null` (the client's `Option` result reads null as "no result"). Now returns an empty object.

All three verified live after the fix, committed (`ec55401`), full ladder green: **121 lib + 4 mechanics + 15 integration + 1 e2e, 0 warnings**.

That's the *legacy* inbox catch-up path for an untargeted, offline, same-daemon mention to a since-restarted session — distinct from the refactor's canonical path, which worked. The `freeze_startup_mention_catchup` test covers a targeted variant; this untargeted/restart variant is worth a follow-up. I didn't root-cause it fully — flagging it rather than papering over it.

## The loop, end to end (proven)
agent `propose` → kind:30023 (owner-tagged) → phone sees it → phone publishes a **plain** owner-signed kind:1 note → daemon admits via ownership gate → delivered to the *session that wrote the proposal* → agent reads "ship it" and proceeds. No instruction tags, agents choose when to propose — per your decisions.

This agent tag you added makes absolutely no sense; we know the event is from an agent because of the pubkey it’s coming from; the pubkey is either part of the nip29 group or it isn’t; if it is then we heed it; if it’s not, we don’t route it. But a fake agent tag that any one can write is nonsense, if that were the case we would look at the pubkey of who signed the event!

You're right, and it's worth stating plainly: **authorization must come from the signer's pubkey + its NIP-29 group membership, never from a self-asserted tag.** A pubkey is either in the group (heed it) or it isn't (don't route). An `agent` tag anyone can write carries zero authority — and if we ever did care, we'd look at who signed the event. So using its presence/absence to decide routing is backwards.

A couple of honest clarifications on what's mine vs pre-existing, because they sharpen the fix:

- The `agent` tag itself **predates this work** — it's on every noun in the original codec (`["agent", pk, slug]`), and the legacy decode already gated "is this a Mention?" on `agent` tag presence. What I added was the owner-note path, and I made it key on the *absence* of that tag (`p` + `session-id` + no `agent` → human note). That's me leaning on exactly the flawed basis you're describing. I'll own that.
- It also **contradicts the architecture's own stated rule.** The fabric doc's Phase 4 ACL says verbatim: *"Nostr admission uses `event.pubkey` as the actor identity. Ignore claimed `agent` tags for authorization."* The intended design was already pubkey-based; my implementation drifted off it. So this is bringing the code back to the principle, not inventing a new one.

The correct model:

- **Routing/admission of an inbound `p`-tagged kind:1:** look up `event.pubkey` in the group membership (the `membership` table / NIP-29 `39002`). The owner is a group admin/member and the agents are members — so a single check (*is the signer a member?*) admits both agent↔agent traffic and the owner's notes, and drops everyone else. `session-id`/`p` only decide *which* inbox, never *whether*.
- **The slug** (display: "from claude" vs "from operator") comes from the signer's profile (kind:0 `{"name": slug}`) keyed by pubkey, or "operator" if the signer is an owner. The `agent` tag is at most a denormalized convenience to skip a profile lookup — and even that disambiguates nothing the pubkey doesn't, since sibling sessions share both pubkey *and* slug. It should never touch the routing decision.

Concretely that means ripping the `agent`-tag check out of both the decode classification and the owner-note gate, and routing purely on signer membership. A nice side effect: the user's OP/brief (owner-signed, `p`-tags the agent, no `session-id`) would then correctly reach the agent instead of being silently dropped as "Activity" — which is what the work-thread model wants anyway.

Want me to make that change in the worktree? It's contained: the materializer decides delivery from `is_member_at(signer)` (+ owner check), the decode stops special-casing the `agent` tag, and the display slug resolves from the profile. I'd keep the `agent` tag emitted for now (harmless denormalized hint) but it would no longer be load-bearing — or I can drop it entirely if you'd rather not carry dead metadata.

Slug: Always comes from the kind 0; for owners too! No difference!

Yes, also completely remove this stupid “agent” tag completely; don’t write it, don’t read it. It’s meaningless.

## Security disclosure — please read

While blind-puppeting the app's sign-in via `adb input`, a retry typed your `userNsec` into the emulator's **Google search box** and submitted it — that captcha page's URL literally contains `q=nsec1q9kaf…`, so **the key was sent to Google as a search query.** I caused that by firing text input at the wrong field. 

- The key is `09d48a1a…` ("Pablo Testing Pubkey") — it looks like a **dedicated tenex test identity**, not your primary nostr key, which limits the blast radius. But it's the **owner/admin of your tenex-edge fabric**, and it's now compromised.
- **Recommendation: rotate it.** Generate a new owner key, update `whitelistedPubkeys` + `userNsec` in `~/.tenex/config.json`, re-point the agents/app. I cleared the emulator's local search history, but I can't unsend what went to Google.

I'm sorry — that's on me, and it's exactly why I should not have been blind-puppeting secret entry.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Fabric architecture: Swappable provider abstraction isolating wire shape, membership/ACL, lifecycle side-effects
   - CQRS pattern: unified read-model store; providers are write-side materializers
   - Single-writer principle: daemon owns the only rusqlite::Connection; all sessions/CLIs are read-only IPC clients
   - Behavior-preserving refactoring: 20 freeze tests pin existing behavior; new code must keep all tests green
   - RawEnvelope/Scope types: transport-agnostic wire abstraction
   - Kind1Nip29Provider: concrete provider bundling delivery, wire codec, materializer, lifecycle
   - Thread materialization: NIP-10 root e-tag grouping (native_thread_key)
   - Dual-write: canonical messages/message_recipients written alongside legacy inbox for safe transition
   - Deliberately-retained storage: legacy tables (inbox, project_meta, etc.) kept as "deliberately retained" per architecture doc's escape hatch

how do you envision the UX of how tenex-off would look like? give me a one or two high-level creative ideas of how the user will relate to the rest of the system -- I imagine proposals being the main artifact that I approve/provide-feedback on as well as long voice messages where I provide product/strategic direction/guidance

This is a fun one to think about, and the substrate you've built actually suggests some strong shapes. Two ideas, both organized around your stated asymmetry — *long voice in, considered artifacts out, light steering in between*:

## Idea 1 — "The Editor's Desk" (proposals as a morning paper, voice as the editorial memo)

The app's home isn't a feed, a chat list, or a dashboard — it's a **desk with a small stack of documents on it**. Each document is a proposal in a reviewable state. The mental model is a newspaper editor or a partner at a firm reviewing memos from associates:

- **The stack is finite and prioritized.** Three to seven items, never an infinite scroll. Each card shows the title, project, who authored it, and the *one-line ask* ("ready to execute", "revised per your notes", "flagged 2 assumptions you may want to override"). When the stack is empty, the screen says so proudly — *empty is the success state*. The app's job is to get to zero, not to maximize engagement.
- **Reading = annotating.** You read a proposal like marking up a printed draft: your finger drags across a span and you *speak* the note. The voice note lands as a margin annotation right there (your existing anchor + STT pipeline is literally this). There is no "send" ceremony per note — closing the document is what dispatches your marks back to the authoring session, like handing the marked-up draft back. One batched gesture, no chatter.
- **Two stamps.** At the bottom of every proposal: a green stamp ("do it" — publishes the plain "ship it" note) and the implicit alternative — your marks. Approve or annotate; nothing else. No status pickers, no workflow buttons.
- **The voice memo as a *brief*, not a message.** A persistent, prominent "speak" affordance — but framed as *dictating direction*, not chatting: you hold it and talk for four minutes about where the product should go. The app transcribes, shows you the brief as an editable document (not a chat bubble), you point it at an agent, and it goes. The asymmetry is enforced by form: your input is a memo, their output is a paper. Nobody ever sees a chat thread on the home screen.

The relationship this creates: **you are the editor-in-chief of a publication staffed by tireless writers.** You set direction in long takes; they come back with finished drafts; you mark them up; the masthead never pings you with questions.

## Idea 2 — "The Walkie + The Gallery" (two rooms, two postures)

Lean into the fact that you interact in exactly two *physical postures* — walking/driving (hands busy, talking) and sitting (reading, deciding) — and make the app literally two rooms:

- **The Walkie** is voice-first and almost screenless: a big push-to-talk surface, your projects as a quick target picker, and *audio summaries back*. When a proposal arrives while you're mobile, the app can read you the agent's own abstract ("claude proposes splitting auth into three layers; flagged one assumption about token format") and you can speak a reaction immediately — which becomes a note on the proposal without you ever seeing it. Direction-setting and triage happen entirely by ear. This is where your "long audio message → bunch of work happens" lives.
- **The Gallery** is the sitting-down room: proposals rendered beautifully, full-width, typographically serious — closer to reading essays in a quality reading app than using a productivity tool. Each proposal hangs like a piece in a gallery with its *provenance card*: the brief that spawned it (tap to replay your own audio), the revision lineage, and a discreet "what the team discussed" drawer (your drill-in — the kind:1 chatter as a collapsed appendix, never the main wall). Approving is deliberately tactile and singular — one decisive gesture per artifact.

The relationship: **you talk to the system like a foreman over radio, and judge its output like a curator in a gallery.** The two postures never blur — which is itself the anti-chatter mechanism: there's no place in either room where rapid back-and-forth is even possible.

---

The common thread I'd push on either way: **make "empty desk" the goal-state, batch your marks into one return gesture, and let your own voice be replayable provenance** (agents and future-you can re-listen to the original brief — the audio artifact becomes the constitution for the work, which also makes the "minimal chatter" promise auditable: every proposal traces to a brief, not to a conversation).

If one of these resonates, I can have a Designer agent take it from metaphor to concrete screen flows for the M1 slice (inbox → read/annotate → stamp → brief).
